sentences.text: Yes.
Because we-- when we were in-- working in forced labor, there were Jews from other camps.
And they told us, one group was sent off-- we asked for some of our people from our town, said yes, he was killed there.
The German-- [NON-ENGLISH], for instance, where many from out were killed.
Forced labors was also bridge building.
And after finishing, the German killed all.
Women I saw, women and men.
I didn't see children.
Perhaps there were.
You see, we-- we were brought, the group, it was darkening, you see.
We didn't see alive except the militia, Germans.
You see, there are some in black uniform.
I assume they were SS or whatever, and the local militia, and soldier-- but German soldier, you see.
Not really.
We knew in the [?
Pechora ?] there is execution very serious.
But we didn't know is it's a death camp as such.
We were thinking they brought from all the [INAUDIBLE], for instance, in [NON-ENGLISH] they killed people.
They had Romanian political prisoners there.
That was a Romanian occupied territory.
Because between-- Transnistria is between Bug and Nyester.
You see, this part was called Transnistria, the other part of the Nyester.
It was under Romanian government.
It was Professor Alexianu, which was our governor.
It was the government, and he was hanged after the war.
And he had a cabinet is different in [INAUDIBLE], which was one for trade, one for industry.
The one which often appeared in our camp was the-- what is called the minister of industry.
It was, by name, [?
Popsecu, ?] which was all to hanged afterwards, executed after the war when the Russians occupied Bucharest.
He was--
Heradu ?] but, I-- I-- I don't-- I was trying to find.
It perhaps will appear afterwards in the--
Liberation.
Yes, the liberation of Cernauti.
I-- my grandfather and my relatives, when I came-- oh, are you still alive?
Because some had returned from Transnistria.
They paid somebody money, I don't know.
And they brought new that they have seen my late father, you see.
My late father was also in connection with the underground.
And he was-- they liked him there, you see, the old devil.
He organizing this.
Beginning of April-- oh no, later.
In April, 1944--
--not in Cernauti, not to Cernauti because they were not permitted.
The Russian had their system.
You were not permitted to stay were you come.
You had to go where you were born.
It happened my father was born in Strozhnitz, so he-- I-- he was sent to Strozhnitz I was in Strozhnitz organizing the bank at the time.
So they occupied the houses, and other Jewish family, which came back.
And they lived there.
No.
But she was allowed to be--
My father was in Cernauti.
Not, but my father, he was the head of the--
And she was allowed to be with him because--
He had to.
--he was born there.
Yes.
He had to my sister.
All the time.
Yes.
They stayed.
They didn't know, and I didn't know what happened to them.
You see--
--you could not write a letter and ask.
They were helped.
You see, they were so loved by the locals, and the horse [INAUDIBLE] gave them a carriage with horses, with a driver, with two sacks of flour, and some potatoes, and [INAUDIBLE] goods.
So had food, and they sent them home.
Ended, yes.
The-- the Russians has liberated [?
Losova? ?]
They were-- in-- in-- in March, was-- the region was liberated by the Red Army.
And they there, now they were free.
They could move.
My mother was a full-- and she went back to about six stone.
My sister could hardly walk.
He was always-- I mean always in deportation he was not physically-- not strong, but mentally very agile.
And there he organized the partisans there in the group-- gave them plans and this how to go, how to make ambushes, and this.
The other group, not mine.
They were always moving around because, you see, they Ukrainian received the Germans with bread and sword.

But soon, when the German made the greatest mistake they could have made-- they started pressing on them to give the food, give-- or to put them to labor, and then to take their young people to Germany for work.
That finished them off.
They turned around.
Not.
I was not in the Red Army.
I was organizer as commissar for the reorganization of the state bank, cost bank, in the liberated territory.
Not.
I was in partisan until they called in the army.
I had-- because this job, they call it in Russian [RUSSIAN].
I was necessary job, you see.
So they didn't take me in the army.
Only two months later I was take in the army, called up.
The 16th of June exactly.
In the [INAUDIBLE]--
I told you, to-- from Strozhnitz I was sent to Cernauti.
Cernauti I had the HQ is this battalion where you sent groups to the army.
I-- as a matter of fact, I was in jail also one day.
If you're interesting, I could tell you why-- a Russian jail.
I stole 500 soldiers.
I was with these battalions.
I had to-- every-- every unit which needed soldiers, they came 200 solider, a 100 soldiers, so on.
It was a unit, which needed 500 soldiers.
And I was already in connection with the other unit to go.
And I sent them off with this other unit outside the town.
I took them to the station sent them off, for when coming back they were searching for me.
This [INAUDIBLE] a partisan with a-- goes with a sub-machine gun around.
And he took the men out of town where they disappeared.
I have sent them off to the unit.
They were supposed to go to [NON-ENGLISH] for work.
They were Ukrainian, [NON-ENGLISH].
They were for the military industry.
And I sent them off to the front because they asked me to help them.
And then I was called, also, when I arrested I told them Stalin told to [INAUDIBLE].
Comrad Stalin told all for the front, all for victory.
So I did my share.
They couldn't say, but Stalin said it's not-- it's wrong.
I went to Kiev, was sent to Kiev there to-- where they had this camp for-- training camp for the front.
I came there.
I was only three days.
And they-- I was hungry.
And I was told that you hardly got to work to eat.
On the day they marched-- marched forward there were march training.
I didn't like it.
They learned what I already did [INAUDIBLE] practice.
And so, I asked for-- to be sent to the front, [INAUDIBLE] to the front.
They send you-- me with the other group to a unit.
I came with [NON-ENGLISH] where I met the unit.
And with this unit I went opposite [INAUDIBLE] in the forest where they're assembling for attack on [INAUDIBLE].
And there happens the attack in the night.
I got lost.
And I was recruited to [INAUDIBLE].
There's infantry on the tank, and they break through, turn around, and the-- we start shooting [INAUDIBLE].
They're the tank crew.
Yes.
And on the tank, holdings [INAUDIBLE] they are soldiers.
Nothing.
I was a soldier, private.
And in-- thank to my brilliant idea in [INAUDIBLE] they made me sergeant, straight sergeant.
And then they sent me over to the special unit which was formed near Vladimir [?
Volensky. ?]
It's a special commando unit.
We had everything-- [INAUDIBLE] to mine link-- everything.
Every--
Up to the end of the war and after it.
Was 9th of May-- 9th of May 1990-- '45.
In Liebnitz in Germany.
But on the 10th we went over to Czechoslovakia fighting [INAUDIBLE] Field Marshall Shorner, the Shorner group was there.
And then it was back in Germany, and I was a time in Vienna with Allied Commission.
And then went on the way to Japan.
I came to [?
Kolwan. ?] There is a big station that's in Russia already, Ukraine.
And there they had to change from the really small spurs to large spurs, you know.
Russians have wider [INAUDIBLE].
I was there, and probably they heard that I'm coming.
The Emperor of Japan capitulated.
It's not like you see in the-- in the schoolbooks it is was the bomb.
A captain.
I was already captain.
Guards captain.
Nobody asked you.
They didn't ask you.
Oh yes.
You see, it was-- I will tell you an example.
When I was already sergeant and on the move, we had to cross from the Russian territory-- that is called Soviet territory-- into independent Polish territory.
My-- my commanding officer was an engineer major, [?
Peterski. ?] And he told me giving charge of the column.
There were clocks and there were sanitary part.
And he told me, you be careful-- there are two Jews here-- when we cross the border that they don't run away.
[INAUDIBLE] to run away, which he told me.
I knew there were two Ukrainian Jews, which, poor man, one was completely deaf.
But they kept them in the services, you see.
And you didn't make yourself known to them.
Oh yes.
Then I was already-- I wasn't afraid to be a Jew, you see.
I told this [INAUDIBLE] and one was from Vinnytsia.
He was a tailor and worked as a tailor.
You know, they had [INAUDIBLE] repairing uniforms and--
Yeah, the officer?
No.
He didn't know.
But these Jews I knew already they are Jews.
I spoke with them, and I told them.
You see, we already had to go even [INAUDIBLE]-- it's hard to understand.
The Russians don't give you a map to go.
They said to go, I remember, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was a place.
Where is this?
You go west, you will hear after the artillery fire.
That is the front.
And there you will ask, if you see military police, you will ask.
And you will come.
We went through, it was terrible.
It was shortly after the German pulled out.
There were still people hanging on the trees.
And from villages where only the chimneys part were left.
And the whole house was burned down.
They were mostly wooden houses were burned down and this.
And you went after the sound of the front.
When we were coming, I knew in the direction of the San we have to go, towards the San The river San.
And luckily, I had the-- my letters from my guards from this [INAUDIBLE] front.
The girls, and soldiers, the clerks-- all they're armed of course, and they knew how to use a weapon.
And so we moved on.
It's were dangerous territory because of the remnants of the German army still there in the forest.
And they were Poles and Ukrainians which fought on the side of the Germans.
Then they are attacking us.
Not.
I did not know about the camps, you see.
I didn't have with whom to speak.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This from witness as a Jew-- I don't remember just in the moment the name.
He told me his family has-- was killed.
He was in the army, and the family was killed by Germans that he knew.
They capitulate, so I did not go.
So I ask-- they asked me-- first, I formally-- on the way, I popped over to see my parents.
Because in going to Japan, I say when will I return, if I return-- if I am alive?
So I made myself an order out, took another officer with me, and we went.
I had luggage.
It was for Czechoslovakia, on the way, Czechoslovakia, Poland to Krakow there.
And I took-- I bought in Moravska Ostrava-- tell you the town-- I bought needle, I bought things of-- which you can use in barter.
And I took it with my colleague to schlep and to there on the roof.
We went on the train, the roof, so [INAUDIBLE] to Cernauti there, my uncle, which was not deported.
And from there we went with a truck to-- just jumped on a truck and went to Strozhnitz.
And you can imagine, suddenly then I appeared.
Not.
In my own name.
You see, I was ordered demobilized from Strozhnitz.
There at the bank I had my own name, you see.
I returned.
I will tell you.
[INAUDIBLE] where [INAUDIBLE] which I had many [INAUDIBLE], and dark hair I had that I wasn't grey.
And I learnt also a bit of Georgian, [INAUDIBLE] speaking.
So some called Singerian, some Singer, but they some [INAUDIBLE] one they were thinking.
Didn't me.
I always did my job, you see.
Nobody liked to go in a reconnaissance over.
I was dropped in behind the enemy lines, you again.
Again, it was a job which I could do, and [INAUDIBLE] I had [INAUDIBLE] give [INAUDIBLE] they were attacking headquarters for my group, specialist group.
They-- they're attacking headquarter.
They went and stormed, sometime they're killed, some of ours.
And found the headquarter and stole-- brought papers back you see.
Lot of cyphers and numbers.
And it turned out it was a tank-- a tank unit, armored unit, which before the war was a cavalry unit.
And the Germans, they're precision had the list of their hay and all the supplies which they had-- how many kilos for so many horses.
They were thinking it's a code, and brought it under danger back.
So my job, when it was going out, was leading them.
And-- and certain [INAUDIBLE] document was-- was of interest.
We had more-- very few which were specialists.
One was garroting the German guards and to enter there, and other opened every safe which was Percival.
He has sensitive finger.
He was from prison, liberated especially for the job.
And my second in command was a-- the secretary was a young communist of the unit, commissar, Alexander [?
Naza ?] Sasha [?
Soloviov, ?] which was a very good friend of mine.
He had a bullet under the-- under the heart and followed yet to stay on.
Leave, which was self-organized.
I had to back to my unit.
And as this was already finished the war with this, we were put to fight the partisan against us.
Because this was Ukrainian which fought on the side of the German.
Now, they were in the-- with remnants of the German army, which could not join their unit.
Some escaped from prisoner of war camp, Russian prisoner of war camp.
German soldiers and officers, and they formed units, fighting against us, attacking us the other way now.
And so I was in Volhinya.
Our headquarter was in Rovno, and I was fighting-- our unit was fighting them.
Oh, until about-- it was in already '46.
In winter '46.
And then I asked to be sent to Kuril Islands.
They needed officers in Kuril Islands.
North of Japan, the islands, which are still under Soviet Russian [INAUDIBLE].
Yes.
It was triple salary, six months.
You had six months off.
I made the plan to come because our commandment was the Allied Commission in Japan.
And then I had in Japan a friend which were university, Abram Vishnica.
I told you previously, and I mentioned he was there.
And I hoped perhaps he's still there so I get from there to America, or to Palestine, as I wanted to come out of the Soviet Union.
In particular, I got-- and both my parents were repatriated is Romanian citizen to Romania.
No, no.
Nothing.
They-- they were not in Cernauti.
They were immediately sent off to Strozhnitz they had to go, where my father lived.
And then-- then they repatriated.
So [INAUDIBLE] from is they didn't want to stay under the Soviet.
It was not very good.
It was part of the Ukraine now.
It was Bucovina, but it's changed from [INAUDIBLE].
'46 I think, April.
It's [NON-ENGLISH].
It's in the South was Bucovina, [NON-ENGLISH].
And there they lived by [INAUDIBLE] you know, as American [INAUDIBLE].
There was a Jewish community from local, which had also returned from deportation.
And one helped the other.
Not.
No, my father died the day of his [INAUDIBLE], he died, the 24th 1946, December 1946.
They [INAUDIBLE].
My mother died a few months later.
My sister was left alone from the family.
And she came on Alia in '50-- '50-- 1950.
She came to Italy to me.
Not.
I didn't go because if you ask a Russian you want to go in this direction, he sends you in the opposite direction.
You see, they couldn't 't me-- not send me to Germany because they had-- the order was given, secret order-- I didn't know about it-- not to send Jews or westerners.
We were considered westerner for the newly occupied territory '40, which were occupied from Poland or from Bucovina.
Not to send Germany because they run over to-- to the capitalist world.
So I was sent to Romania.
And in the Romania, I was the Allied Commission.
First, I was searching for my parents.
I found them in [NON-ENGLISH].
I-- it was about a month too with them.
And I used my-- you had to earn money because-- so I worked putting mines in a stone work.
And then, I had to go back to Bucharest.
There I met to announce myself ready for duty.
There with the Allied Commission there were friends of mine from school, which came back as officers in the Eighth Army.
Some of them, Fishman in particular, was my school friend, primary school.
Afterwards we were friend.
He was parachuted in occupied Romania as British officer.
And he told me just the man who could help us.
And I joined up with them.
Officially, I was a Russian officer, could move around, because they as British officer could not move so easily around [INAUDIBLE].
I could move and began to move weapon towards endangered Jewish places.
So I came in [INAUDIBLE].
I came in different places, I came there.
And then, I was sent off as emissary officially of the [INAUDIBLE] Jewish Congress.
You know, it was all for different name.
But officially, it's Russian.
Then I was sent because they started organizing military unit, training, giving training.
So that was my job to go.
Young people, which came back from DP camps.
For Israel, to go in Alia to Palestine as it was.
I was in civil already.
I went-- I-- you see, I was officially on a leave of duty until I had to come back, until they called me.
I arranged this, you see.
But I didn't come back.
I had my other jobs, you see, and I was moving around.
But still, when needed, I put the Russian uniform on, make myself the papers.
We had all specialist which could falsify even Stalin's signature.
Since I moved-- I had papers to move around, orders.
But I went to Lugoj, where a friend of mine had organized two groups.
They were one agricultural and one industrial, which prepared for work, training, work training.
Here, you had two groups.
In Romania.
In the [NON-ENGLISH] near the Yugoslav border.
Lugoj.
